skill.name: debugging-websocket-issues
skill.description: Troubleshoot WebSocket frame errors in Node.js applications, including conflicting upgrade handlers, RSV1 problems, compression mismatches, and raw frame inspection.
skill.summary: Diagnose WebSocket frame header and RSV1 errors in Node.js services.
skill.author: AgentWorkforce

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many Markdown backtick examples as shell execution and weak cryptography, but the file is documentation for WebSocket debugging. The reviewed content contains no runnable scripts, no secret access, no data exfiltration, and no prompt injection attempt. One hardcoded HTTP URL appears inside an example URL parser fallback and is not an outbound network request.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported external command execution, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but the evidence is Markdown inline code and TypeScript troubleshooting examples. The file contains no executable installer, script, shell command, secret access, or autonomous action.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged tokens are embedded in prose, tables, or fenced TypeScript examples. Semantic review found documentation only, with no code that the skill runs.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Used as Local Parser Base
low_findings.description: Line 61 contains a hardcoded http URL inside a URL constructor fallback for parsing request paths. It uses the request host as a base string and does not perform a network request or transmit data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears in documentation as a parser base for request.url. There is no fetch, socket client, webhook, or external endpoint behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation-Only WebSocket Debugging Snippets
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes TypeScript snippets that inspect socket bytes and route WebSocket upgrades. These examples can affect application behavior if copied, but they are not executed by the skill itself.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The snippets are legitimate for WebSocket debugging, but they touch low-level socket handling. Users should review them before applying in production.

content.value_statement: WebSocket frame errors can hide conflicts between upgrade handlers, compression settings, and raw HTTP responses. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical checks that isolate the failure source.
actual_capabilities: Explains common causes of invalid WebSocket frame headers.
Shows how multiple WebSocketServer instances can conflict on one HTTP server.
Recommends noServer routing for multiple WebSocket paths.
Provides raw frame inspection guidance for Node.js ws sockets.
Maps hex byte patterns to common WebSocket symptoms.
Lists verification checks after fixing upgrade handling.
limitations: Focuses on Node.js and the ws library, not every WebSocket implementation.
Requires access to server logs or code to confirm root causes.
Does not replace protocol-level security review for production gateways.
Debug snippets should be adapted before use in high-traffic services.

use_cases.title: Fix Node.js ws Upgrade Conflicts
use_cases.description: Use the checklist to find duplicate upgrade handlers and route paths through one controlled upgrade listener.

use_cases.title: Diagnose RSV1 and Compression Errors
use_cases.description: Compare frame symptoms against compression settings and per-message deflate behavior.

use_cases.title: Support Incident Triage
use_cases.description: Collect focused evidence from logs, close codes, raw bytes, and upgrade headers during a WebSocket outage.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Error
prompt_templates.prompt: I see Invalid frame header in a Node.js WebSocket app. Explain the likely causes and the first checks I should run.

prompt_templates.title: Review Upgrade Routing
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my WebSocket upgrade handling for multiple ws paths. Look for conflicting WebSocketServer instances and suggest a noServer routing design.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Raw Frame Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Here are my first frame bytes, close code, and upgrade headers. Determine whether this is raw HTTP, compression mismatch, or another WebSocket issue.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Incident Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production-safe WebSocket incident checklist for RSV1 errors, duplicate upgrade handlers, compression negotiation, and post-fix verification.

output_examples.input: A service logs WS_ERR_UNEXPECTED_RSV_1 after adding a second WebSocket path.
output: Likely cause: both WebSocket servers receive the same upgrade event.
Recommended action: route upgrades manually with noServer mode.
Verification: confirm no raw HTTP response appears in received frame bytes.

output_examples.input: The first received bytes decode to HTTP after a successful WebSocket connection.
output: Likely cause: an unmatched upgrade handler wrote an HTTP error to the upgraded socket.
Recommended action: audit every server upgrade listener and destroy unknown paths cleanly.
Verification: repeat the connection and confirm frame bytes match WebSocket opcodes.

output_examples.input: A client disconnects with code 1006 and no close reason.
output: Likely cause: abnormal server-side closure or handshake abort.
Recommended action: check abortHandshake calls, path matching, and extension negotiation.
Verification: compare sent payload sizes with received message sizes after the fix.

best_practices: Use one upgrade listener to route multiple WebSocket paths.
Log upgrade headers and close codes before changing protocol settings.
Disable or verify compression when investigating RSV1 frame errors.
anti_patterns: Do not attach several WebSocketServer instances to the same HTTP server without routing.
Do not assume every RSV1 error is a compression bug.
Do not leave raw socket instrumentation enabled in normal production traffic.

faq.question: What WebSocket problem does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets invalid frame header errors, RSV1 errors, abnormal closures, and raw HTTP data reaching a WebSocket client.

faq.question: Does it execute any commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill is a Markdown troubleshooting guide with TypeScript examples for users to review.

faq.question: Why does it mention noServer mode?
faq.answer: noServer mode lets one upgrade listener choose the correct WebSocketServer for each path.

faq.question: Can this help with browser clients?
faq.answer: Yes, when the server uses Node.js ws and the browser reports frame or closure errors.

faq.question: Is the hardcoded http URL a network call?
faq.answer: No. It is used as a base string in a URL parsing example, not as an outbound request.

faq.question: What evidence should I collect before using it?
faq.answer: Collect close codes, server upgrade listeners, compression settings, headers, and the first received frame bytes.
